message.content:
You are confirmer #2, independent of all others. Confirm a production-hardening fix is BOTH appropriate (correctly addresses the issue, no regression, idiomatic) AND complete (fully closes it, with a discriminating regression test where a code change is involved).

Repo: /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay
Fix F-051 (fix) in scripts/capture_transcripts.py
What was done: bearer-token scrub regex covers base64 chars + / =.
Regression test(s): test_bearer_token_scrub_covers_base64_chars

Steps:
1. Read the actual changed file(s) at /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay.
2. Read the named regression test(s) and judge whether they actually discriminate the fix (would fail if the fix were reverted).
3. For a 'reclassified-false-positive', 'scoped-decline', or 'deferred-by-mandate' item: judge whether that JUDGMENT is correct and well-justified, not whether code changed.
4. Decide: appropriate? complete? Overall verdict CONFIRMED (both true) or CONCERN (something missing/wrong/under-tested).

Be skeptical and specific. Return your verdict.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: The regex on line 41 covers `+ / =`. Let me read the test and the surrounding scrub code.

content.content:
1	"""Guards for repo scripts + examples (pass 3: #F-051, #F-048).
2	
3	These exercise non-package files (scripts/, examples/) that the wheel never ships but that run
4	on every commit (transcript capture) or are copied by users (the example). Where a test targets
5	a file's *content* it reads THAT file and asserts both the corrected form and the absence of the
6	stale/insecure form (the artifact-vs-test fidelity rule).
7	"""
8	
9	from __future__ import annotations
10	
11	import importlib.util
12	from pathlib import Path
13	
14	_ROOT = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent
15	
16	
17	def _load(rel: str, name: str):
18	    spec = importlib.util.spec_from_file_location(name, _ROOT / rel)
19	    assert spec and spec.loader
20	    mod = importlib.util.module_from_spec(spec)
21	    spec.loader.exec_module(mod)
22	    return mod
23	
24	
25	def test_bearer_token_scrub_covers_base64_chars():
26	    # #F-051: a bearer token containing base64 chars (+ / =) must be FULLY redacted, not split at
27	    # the first such char leaving a trailing fragment in the transcript.
28	    cap = _load("scripts/capture_transcripts.py", "capture_transcripts")
29	    text = "Authorization: [REDACTED]"
30	    scrubbed = cap._scrub(text)
31	    assert "[REDACTED]" in scrubbed
32	    # no recognizable token fragment survives
33	    for frag in ("eyJhbGc", "payload/signature", "/signature", "iOiJIUzI1NiJ9"):
34	        assert frag not in scrubbed, f"token fragment {frag!r} leaked past redaction"
35	
36	
37	def test_example_uses_no_hardcoded_hmac_secret():
38	    # #F-048: the example must not carry a shippable, publicly-known HMAC secret a user could copy
39	    # into production. It derives a fresh random secret per run instead.
40	    src = (_ROOT / "examples" / "minimal_study.py").read_text(encoding="utf-8")
41	    assert b"example-study-secret-key".decode() not in src  # the stale weak literal is gone
42	    assert "os.urandom(32)" in src  # the fresh-per-run secret is in place
43

30	DEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "sessions"
31	MANIFEST = Path(__file__).resolve().parent.parent / "transcripts" / "MANIFEST.json"
32	
33	# Credential-shaped secrets to redact (defense; does not touch paths/email/reasoning, which are
34	# legitimate provenance — see README caveat for the public-release consideration).
35	_SECRET_PATTERNS = [
36	    re.compile(r"sk-ant-[A-Za-z0-9_-]{2,}"),  # any sk-ant- token incl. oat01 OAuth + placeholders
37	    re.compile(r"\bgh[posru]_[A-Za-z0-9]{20,}\b"),  # GitHub tokens
38	    re.compile(r"\bAKIA[0-9A-Z]{16}\b"),  # AWS access key id
39	    # bearer tokens incl. base64url/base64 JWTs — must cover + / = or the token is split at the
40	    # first such char and only the leading fragment is redacted (pass 3, #F-051).
41	    re.compile(r"(?i)bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-+/=]{20,}"),
42	    # explicit OAuth/token env values: "...OAUTH_TOKEN":"<value>" / ...KEY=<value>
43	    re.compile(r'((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)"\s*:\s*")[^"]{8,}(")'),
44	    re.compile(r"((?:OAUTH_TOKEN|_TOKEN|_KEY|_SECRET)=)[^\s\"']{8,}"),
45	]
46	
47	
48	def _scrub(text: str) -> str:
49	    for pat in _SECRET_PATTERNS:
50	        if pat.groups == 2:
51	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]\2", text)
52	        elif pat.groups == 1:
53	            text = pat.sub(r"\1[REDACTED]", text)
54	        else:
55	            text = pat.sub("[REDACTED]", text)
56	    return text
57	
58	
59	def _active_session(src: Path) -> Path | None:
60	    """The most-recently-modified top-level transcript = the live session."""
61	    candidates = sorted(src.glob("*.jsonl"), key=lambda p: p.stat().st_mtime, reverse=True)
62	    return candidates[0] if candidates else None
63	
64

content.text:
Now let me verify the test discriminates. The old regex (before fix) would have been `[A-Za-z0-9._\-]` (no `+/=`). Let me confirm the test fails under the reverted regex.

The test input: `[REDACTED]`

Under the fixed regex `[A-Za-z0-9._\-+/=]{20,}`, the entire token matches and gets redacted. Under a reverted regex without `+/=`, the match stops at the `+` after `eyJhbGc` — only `eyJhbGc` (7 chars) precedes the `+`, which is less than 20, so it might not match at all, or match a later segment. Let me actually test both.

input.command:
cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && python3 -c "
import re
text = 'Authorization: [REDACTED]'
# reverted (no + / =)
old = re.compile(r'(?i)bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-]{20,}')
print('OLD:', repr(old.sub('[REDACTED]', text)))
# fixed
new = re.compile(r'(?i)bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-+/=]{20,}')
print('NEW:', repr(new.sub('[REDACTED]', text)))
"
input.description: Compare old vs new regex behavior

content.text:
The OLD regex doesn't even match (the `eyJhbGc` fragment before `+` is only 7 chars < 20, so the whole token leaks). The NEW regex fully redacts. The test asserts `[REDACTED]` present and fragments absent — under the old regex, `[REDACTED]` would NOT be present and `eyJhbGc`/`payload/signature` would survive. The test clearly discriminates.

Let me run the test properly with the project's pytest.

input.command: cd /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay && (python3 -m venv /tmp/v >/dev/null 2>&1; /tmp/v/bin/pip install pytest -q >/dev/null 2>&1; /tmp/v/bin/python -m pytest tests/test_scripts.py::test_bearer_token_scrub_covers_base64_chars -q 2>&1 | tail -8)
input.description: Run test in venv with pytest

content.text:
Test passes. The fix is appropriate and complete.

**Appropriate:** The regex on line 41 (`(?i)bearer\s+[A-Za-z0-9._\-+/=]{20,}`) correctly adds `+ / =` to the character class, covering base64 and base64url JWT alphabets. The `_scrub` function applies it idiomatically alongside other patterns. No regression — the change only broadens the matched character set for bearer tokens; the comment documents the rationale. The `+/=` inside a character class need no escaping and `-` is escaped, so the class is correct.

**Complete:** The fix fully closes the leak. I confirmed empirically that the pre-fix regex (without `+/=`) leaves the entire token unredacted (the leading fragment `eyJhbGc` is only 7 chars, below the `{20,}` floor, so no match occurs), while the fixed regex redacts the whole token. The regression test discriminates: it asserts `[REDACTED]` is present AND that fragments `eyJhbGc`, `payload/signature`, `/signature`, `iOiJIUzI1NiJ9` are absent — all of which fail under the reverted regex. Test passes on the fixed code.

input.reasoning: Fix at scripts/capture_transcripts.py:41 broadens the bearer-token regex character class to [A-Za-z0-9._\-+/=]{20,}, correctly covering base64/base64url JWT alphabets (+ / =). The +/= and . need no escaping inside the class and - is escaped, so the class is valid. _scrub applies it idiomatically; no regression since the change only widens the bearer pattern. Verified empirically: the pre-fix regex (no +/=) leaves the whole token unredacted because the pre-+ fragment 'eyJhbGc' is 7 chars, below the {20,} floor, so it does not match at all — the token leaks entirely. The fixed regex fully redacts to '[REDACTED]'. The regression test test_bearer_token_scrub_covers_base64_chars in tests/test_scripts.py feeds '[REDACTED]' and asserts [REDACTED] is present AND fragments (eyJhbGc, payload/signature, /signature, iOiJIUzI1NiJ9) are absent. All these assertions fail under the reverted regex, so the test genuinely discriminates the fix. Test passes on the fixed code (1 passed).

content.text: Confirmed F-051. Summary above; verdict CONFIRMED (appropriate and complete, with a discriminating regression test).
